title: 28 Nehase — St. Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob & The Assembly
saints: The Holy Patriarchs Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob
St. Augustine of Hippo, Bishop and Confessor
narrative:
The twenty-eighth day of Nehase gathers the three great Patriarchs of Israel into the Church's embrace: Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, the fathers of the faith, the ones to whom the promises were sworn, the tent-dwellers who walked with God in the full novelty of a relationship that had never before existed. The Ethiopian Church honors them not merely as historical ancestors of Judaism but as living members of the assembly of the righteous, present now in the fullness of the Promise whose shadow they inhabited on earth. The God who identified Himself to Moses as 'the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob' — and who is, as the Lord said, the God of the living and not the dead — is the same God whom the Ethiopian faithful worship in their liturgies. These three are not strangers but family, not Old Testament figures but members of the eternal household.

Abraham is the father of all who believe: he went out without knowing where he was going, which is the founding act of faith, the first and unrepeatable step into the unknown that all subsequent believers replay in their own way. Isaac was the child of impossibility who became the embodiment of the willingness to be offered — lying on the mountain, he was both the sacrifice and the one who was spared, a double icon of the Lamb of God. Jacob, the wrestler with the Angel, the one who limped away from Peniel with a blessing and a permanent wound, is the ancestor of all those who have been changed by their encounter with the divine. To have met God is to be permanently different; Jacob's limp is the Church's limp, the holy disability of those who have been in the Presence.

The monthly commemoration of St. Moses the Black, the Ethiopian robber-chief turned desert father and priest, is perfectly placed on this day of the Patriarchs. Moses was a man of Africa, of massive physical stature and volcanic temper, who underwent one of the most complete conversions in the Sayings of the Desert Fathers. He became a monk at Scetis, was ordained priest with great resistance from his own sense of unworthiness, and was martyred with his fellow monks by Berber raiders after refusing to flee. His wisdom is among the most quoted in the Apophthegmata: 'Stay in your cell, and your cell will teach you everything.' For the Ethiopian Church he is an ancestor in the faith in a peculiarly intimate sense — he is their kind, their color, their soil — and his holiness is a testimony that the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob is also the God of the Nile and the desert and the dark skin.

St. Augustine of Hippo brings North African Christianity to its highest theological pitch on this same day. Augustine — son of Monica, rhetorician of Carthage, bishop of Hippo, doctor of grace — was formed in the same African soil that formed Moses the Black, though in a different era and tradition. His Confessions are the most intimate record of a human soul's journey to God in the literature of the ancient Church; his City of God is the most comprehensive attempt to read the whole of human history through the lens of the Kingdom. On this twenty-eighth day of Nehase, Abraham the tent-dweller, Isaac the near-sacrifice, Jacob the wrestler, Moses the converted robber, and Augustine the converted rhetorician form an improbable and magnificent chorus of those who were found, changed, and made new by the God who seeks what is lost.
